data.description:
# Publish the review policy: what the LLM-Review and Verified gates mean for contributors

## Why

rebar's blocking `LLM-Review` Gerrit label appears to be the first of its kind in public OSS: research (2024–2026) found no project documenting a blocking LLM merge vote — the Kubernetes ecosystem's 2026 AI-review policy makes AI review advisory-only and emphatically human-authoritative. Novelty raises the documentation bar: contributors will not trust an AI-gated merge process that is not written down. The document's SHAPE is convergent even though the content is novel: Chromium's CQ doc and Zuul's gating doc both name the labels, define each vote value, state bot-vs-human authority, and document the escape hatches.

## What

New `docs/review-policy.md`, contributor-facing (linked from CONTRIBUTING.md and docs/your-first-change.md):

- The two required labels: `Verified` (CI — the exact commands: `make lint && make typecheck && make test`; link the workflow) and `LLM-Review` (what the bot reviews, which model/harness, and that only bots/admins can vote either label — no self-approval, no self-verification).
- Vote-semantics table (Chromium CQ style): what each value means and the productive response — `−1 [LLM-Review: BLOCK — finding]` (real findings in your diff: fix and re-push) vs `−1 [LLM-Review: BLOCK — coverage-gap]` (infra veto, NOT your code) vs `Verified −1` (CI failure: open the linked run; comment `recheck` for flakes). This taxonomy already exists in CLAUDE.md — the work is publishing it outsider-facing.
- Bot vs human authority — the section needing the most care, because it departs from the Kubernetes advisory-only norm; borrow Zuul's "mechanical enforcement" framing to defuse it: (a) a human (the lead maintainer) sets the bot's rubric and can waive or override any finding; (b) the dispute path — resolve the comment thread with written justification, or raise it to the maintainer; (c) the gate supplements, never replaces, maintainer judgment; (d) bypasses are admin-only and auditable.
- Responsibility clause (the Kubernetes-2026 norm, stated credibly by a project that dogfoods agents): the submitter must be able to personally explain their change, AI-assisted or not.
- Framing decision to record: advertise the gate as "required-with-human-override" (the honest form), and note in the doc that the dispute path is v1 and will iterate with the first external contributor — no precedent exists to copy.
- Distinguish explicitly: this doc covers the CODE-review merge gate; the plan-review (ticket) gate is a separate internal-workflow gate and is out of this doc's scope (one clarifying sentence, link to docs/plan-review-gate.md).

## Scope

IN: `docs/review-policy.md` (new), links from CONTRIBUTING.md and your-first-change.md.
OUT: changing gate behavior, thresholds, or criteria; the plan-review gate's own docs; Gerrit ACL changes.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `docs/review-policy.md` present with: both labels defined, the vote-semantics table including the coverage-gap infra-veto distinction, authority/dispute/bypass sections, and the responsibility clause.
- [ ] The no-self-approval fact and the maintainer-override power are both stated plainly.
- [ ] Linked from CONTRIBUTING.md and docs/your-first-change.md.
- [ ] The "required-with-human-override" framing decision and the v1-will-iterate caveat are recorded on the ticket and reflected in the doc.
data.title: Publish the review policy: what the LLM-Review and Verified gates mean for contributors
